title: Central charges of B-branes at non geometric phases
speaker: Mauricio Romo
abstract: Abstract: I'll give an overview of basic concepts about B-branes and their central charges and how they arise in physics and mathematics. In particular I'll present the gauge linear model approach which allows for defining quantities in the full stringy Kahler moduli M of certain Calabi-Yaus (CY). Then, I'll show some examples and motivate an intrinsic definition of the central charges on phases (some sub-regions of M), based on field theory data.

proof_stages.label: GLSM input data and phase quotient
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines a gauged linear sigma model by a gauge group, a representation, an R-charge, a G-invariant superpotential of R-charge two, and complexified FI-theta parameters, then explains the associated critical-locus quotient X_zeta that varies with the parameters.

proof_stages.label: B-branes as matrix factorizations plus contours
proof_stages.summary: A B-brane in the GLSM is specified by an equivariant matrix factorization of W together with a middle-dimensional noncompact Lagrangian contour gamma encoding vector-multiplet boundary conditions.

proof_stages.label: Stringy Kähler moduli and phases
proof_stages.summary: The stringy Kähler moduli is described as (C*)^s with a discriminant removed, whose logarithmic projection has chamber-like phases that may be geometric Calabi-Yau, Landau-Ginzburg/orbifold, hybrid, or more poorly understood non-geometric regions.

proof_stages.label: Phase equivalence and grade-restricted lifts
proof_stages.summary: Because paths in the punctured Kähler moduli connect phases, the corresponding B-brane categories should be equivalent, but the GLSM-to-phase maps are only projections, so one chooses grade-restricted/window subcategories that project equivalently.

proof_stages.label: Windows indexed by argument chambers
proof_stages.summary: On the universal cover of the Kähler moduli, the argument map cuts theta-space into regions T_L, and each such region labels a window category controlling transport in theta as distinct from motion between phases in zeta.

proof_stages.label: Localization formula for the central charge
proof_stages.summary: The central charge of a brane (B,gamma) is presented as a holomorphic, generally multivalued, integral over gamma with Gamma-function factors from matter weights, root factors from the gauge group, and a character f_B encoding the matrix factorization.

proof_stages.label: Admissible contours and convergence criterion for windows
proof_stages.summary: The contour gamma is required to be Lagrangian, deformable from the real contour without crossing poles, asymptotically convergent, and Weyl-invariant in the nonabelian case, and grade restriction is proposed to be equivalent to existence of such admissible contours throughout a theta-window.

proof_stages.label: Origin and asymptotic analysis of the central-charge construction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the integral is obtained by supersymmetric localization on a disk, emphasizes that the hard part is analyzing the effective growth function governing admissibility, and points to new nonabelian path/window phenomena.

examples.name: mirror-side discriminant description
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that when a mirror Calabi-Yau is available, the removed discriminant in the stringy Kähler moduli can be seen as the singular locus in the mirror complex-structure moduli, equivalently through Picard-Fuchs singularities.
examples.engagement_reason: he describes the mirror-side discriminant only as a way to identify delta via singular mirror complex structures/Picard-Fuchs singularities, without computing it for a specific mirror

passes.reason: It identifies the discriminant as the mirror-side singular/critical locus in moduli, a boundary/critical set used explanatorily rather than to refute anything.
the mirror discriminant is the critical/singular locus of degenerate fibers that must be removed from the moduli space.
the mirror-side discriminant is the critical locus of singular/degenerate mirror fibers in moduli, used descriptively rather than adversarially
The mirror-side discriminant is the critical/singular locus in complex-structure moduli where the mirror family degenerates, used only to describe Δ.
standard mirror description of the GLSM discriminant as the mirror complex-structure singular locus, used explanatorily rather than adversarially
The mirror discriminant is the singular-fibre locus removed because it obstructs the naïve all-parameters moduli.
It is the standard mirror-symmetry description of the discriminant locus as singular complex structures/Picard–Fuchs singularities, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
The mirror-side discriminant is used as the critical/singular boundary locus in moduli, not to refute a claim.
It is used only to explain the discriminant via singular mirror fibers, which form a degenerate locus in the mirror family.
the mirror-side discriminant is the critical singular/bad locus in moduli that is removed, used for description rather than refutation
The mirror discriminant is the critical/singular boundary locus in moduli, used as an explanatory description rather than to refute a claim.
The mirror-side discriminant is used to explain the excluded singular/degenerate mirror complex structures, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: quintic hypersurface GLSM
examples.note: The U(1) GLSM on C^6 with weights (1,1,1,1,1,-5) and superpotential p f_5(x) is used to make the abstract GLSM data, the geometric/Landau-Ginzburg phases, the chamber picture, and Orlov equivalence concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he writes the explicit U(1), C^6 weight data and superpotential for the quintic GLSM and then analyzes its moduli/phases and window intervals

passes.reason: The quintic hypersurface GLSM is presented as the standard representative U(1) hypersurface example, not a sporadic, degenerate, extremal, symmetric, or adversarial case.
the quintic GLSM is the standard generic Calabi–Yau hypersurface example rather than a structurally special or adversarial case.
the quintic hypersurface GLSM is presented as the standard representative U(1) Calabi–Yau hypersurface example, not as a failure case
The quintic hypersurface GLSM is presented as the standard U(1) illustrative Calabi–Yau model, not as an obstruction or special classification exception.
the U(1) quintic hypersurface GLSM is the standard representative Calabi–Yau hypersurface example, not structurally sporadic or used to refute a claim
The quintic hypersurface GLSM is the standard smooth Calabi–Yau GLSM example used illustratively.
The quintic hypersurface GLSM is presented as the basic representative example of a Calabi–Yau GLSM phase structure, not as a failure case or structurally sporadic object.
The quintic hypersurface GLSM is the standard U(1) Calabi–Yau example used illustratively for phases and windows.
The U(1) GLSM for a generic smooth quintic is presented as the standard ordinary Calabi-Yau hypersurface example, not as an adversarial case.
the quintic hypersurface GLSM is the standard U(1) smooth Calabi–Yau example used illustratively

The quintic hypersurface GLSM is presented as the standard illustrative Calabi–Yau GLSM with geometric and Landau–Ginzburg phases.

examples.name: Pfaffian-Grassmannian GLSM
examples.note: The nonabelian U(2) Pfaffian-Grassmannian model is presented as the case where the usual one-dimensional Kähler projection hides extra theta-direction behavior, leading to the observed additional window/path phenomenon.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the moduli/window picture to the Pfaffian-Grassmannian GLSM, using G=U(2) to describe its one-dimensional Kahler moduli, repeated singular points, phase categories, and extra path/window phenomenon

passes.reason: The Pfaffian-Grassmannian GLSM is used as a more complicated nonabelian illustrative example of phases/windows, not as a classification exception or counterexample.

the Pfaffian–Grassmannian GLSM is a special nonabelian example within systematic GLSM/Pfaffian–Grassmannian constructions, not a sporadic exception or counterexample
